EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds developed over Utah in association with a cold front on the morning of February 10, with the strongest wind gusts observed over the mountains of northern Utah. Behind the front, gusty canyon winds developed in far southwest Utah during the late afternoon and early evening hours of February 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Reef RAWS recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm entered the state on February 18, bringing heavy snowfall to both valley and mountain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys saw widespread heavy snowfall. Storm total snowfall reports included 25 inches in Sandy, 23 inches in Cottonwood Heights, 17.5 inches in Tooele, 15 inches in Olympus Cove, and 14 inches in Taylorsville. For the calendar day of February 19, Tooele recorded 13 inches of snow, which broke the calendar day record of 8 inches, set in 1945.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system produced significant to heavy snow across portions of eastern Utah mostly within a 24-hour period from Saturday, January 20th to Sunday, January 21st. Snow began falling across northeast Utah Saturday morning and generally spread south-southeast across the region during the day Saturday. Some lower elevation precipitation began as rain on Saturday and then changed over to snow with the passage of a strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snowfall totals generally ranged from 11 to 20 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March came in like a lion across Utah, as a storm system produced strong winds on March 2, followed by widespread heavy snowfall across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts in the U.S. Army Dugway Proving Ground mesonet included 73 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain and 58 mph at the Durand Road sensor. In addition, four semitrailers were blown over on Interstate 80 by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March came in like a lion across Utah, as a storm system produced strong winds on March 2, followed by widespread heavy snowfall across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 80 mph at Tooele, 74 mph at the SR-201 at I-80 sensor, 63 mph at Olympus Hills, 62 mph at Olympus Cove, and 58 mph at the UDOT I-80 @ mp 78 sensor. Damage in this area included several power poles that were blown over in Tooele, and a carport that was detached from a house in Tooele by the wind; this carport was blown into a power line, and both the power line and carport fell into the street. More than 3,000 people in Tooele County and nearly 1,000 people in Salt Lake County lost power during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snowfall on roadways played a factor in a 23 vehicle crash on Interstate 80 that resulted in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snowfall on roadways contributed to a series of car accidents on Interstate 80 at Echo Junction (milepost 172), or near the junction of Interstate 80 and Interstate 84. Five separate crashes occurred on this stretch of roadway, ultimately involving 23 vehicles, of which 12 of those vehicles were semitrailers. The accident closed Interstate 80 in both directions, and led to one fatality and 16 injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted across the far west-central portion of the zone through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of eastern Utah during the month of June. As a result, drought conditions in eastern Utah either persisted or worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions across much of the zone worsened to extreme (D3) status by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brought widespread snowfall to the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brought widespread snowfall to the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 9 inches occurred across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brought widespread snowfall to the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals generally ranged from 3 to 4 inches in the La Sal Mountains and 9 to 14 inches reported in the Abajo Mountains. Monticello received 5 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The progression and eventual merging of two weather disturbances across eastern Utah produced significant snowfall across the La Sal and Abajo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though February was rather active with snowfall events, precipitation remained well below normal for the month over portions of eastern Utah, and this contributed to the already drier than normal conditions prior to February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions that had been in place all winter worsened in mid February when the situation deteriorated to the extreme (D3) drought category across the area. The extreme drought conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface gradient generated by deepening low pressure associated with an approaching Pacific storm combined with strong flow aloft to generate strong surface winds across much of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 68 mph were measured by the Bryson Canyon automated weather station, and up to 63 mph at the Canyonlands Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface gradient generated by deepening low pressure associated with an approaching Pacific storm combined with strong flow aloft to generate strong surface winds across much of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 64 mph were measured at the Randlett weather station, while gusts up to 60 mph occurred at the Fort Duchesne automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred ahead of and with the passage of a cold front across eastern Utah on Monday, April 2nd, which resulted in gusty surface winds across the Eastern Uinta Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 70 mph were measured at both the Duchesne River and Maeser automated weather stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although some moist weather systems moved through the region during the month of March, these systems typically favored the higher terrain. As a result, precipitation remained below normal for many areas, especially in the southeastern Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred across the area. A peak wind gust of 62 mph was measured at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab with sustained wind speeds to 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and strong radiational cooling under clearing skies allowed low temperatures to plummet to below freezing across some of the lower valleys in eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over half of the forecast zone had minimum temperatures down to 25 to 30 degrees F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation remained below normal for many areas, even with some late season winter storms that moved through the region during the month of April. The most notable monthly deficit occurred in southeastern Utah where precipitation was more than half an inch below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific storm system and associated cold front brought significant snowfall to the mountains of eastern Utah and the Eastern Uintah Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of 2018 saw the arrival of a strong cutoff low pressure system into the southern Great Basin. While the northern portion of the trough produced light snowfall over the northern mountains, the cutoff low produced moderate snowfall over the La Sal and Abajo Mountains as well as the lower elevations of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.

Peak wind gusts of 60 MPH and 70 MPH were measured at the Upper P.R. Canyon and Wildhorse RAWS sites, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire on Monument Ridge, about 5 miles north of the Grand County border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monument 2 Fire began due to lightning ignition on Monument Ridge, 5 miles north of the Grand County border and grew to a total of 209 acres before it was contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human caused fire developed during the afternoon of September 15 in Herriman. The fire destroyed two homes before being contained that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A juvenile playing with fireworks sparked a fire in Herriman on the afternoon of September 15. Due to windy conditions and low relative humidity, the fire spread quickly, eventually burning more than 100 acres, destroying two homes, and causing the temporary evacuation of some nearby neighborhoods before being contained during the evening. One firefighter and four police officers were treated for smoke inhalation and heat exhaustion while helping fight the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire about 21 miles north-northwest of Thompson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Trap Fire began due to lightning ignition about 21 miles north-northwest of Thompson, or on the Uintah and Ouray Indian Reservation in northern Grand County. The fire grew to 12,246 acres before it was contained in mid October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire that was likely ignited by humans, burned in the extreme northeast corner of Utah near the intersection of the Utah, Wyoming and Colorado borders.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bender Mountain Fire that was likely ignited by humans, burned in the extreme northeast corner of Utah. The fire grew to 4,116 acres before it was contained.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Trap Fire began due to lightning about 21 miles north-northwest of Thompson on the Uintah and Ouray Indian Reservation in northern Grand County. The fire grew to 12,246 acres before it was contained in mid October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bald Mountain Fire in northern Utah began on August 24 and burned for over a month before being contained. Note that this episode began in August and continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bald Mountain Fire was a lightning caused wildfire that began on August 24. Initially only about 20 acres, it experienced exceptional growth on September 12 due to high winds and low relative humidity, and eventually burned 18,620 acres. The Bald Mountain Fire and nearby Pole Creek Fire led to the evacuation of 6,000 people from 2,000 homes at the height of the incident, as well as caused numerous road closures. The combined costs to fight the Bald Mountain Fire and the Pole Creek Fire were $23.8 million. Note that this event began in August and continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pole Creek Fire in northern Utah began on September 6 and burned for about a month before being contained. Note that this episode continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pole Creek Fire was a lightning caused wildfire that began on September 6. On September 12, high winds and low relative humidity caused the fire to rapidly grow, and it eventually burned 102,190 acres. The Pole Creek Fire and nearby Bald Mountain Fire led to the evacuation of 6,000 people from 2,000 homes at the height of the incident, as well as caused numerous road closures. The combined costs to fight the Pole Creek Fire and Bald Mountain Fire were $23.8 million. Note that this event continued into October.

In the Camelot Resort area, 30 adults and 13 children were trapped by the water and debris, and had to be rescued by law enforcement, while nearly a dozen people were rescued in Timber Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms over central and southern Utah produced flash flooding during the second week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Zion National Park produced the largest flash flood at the park since 2011, with the flow volume of the North Fork of the Virgin River increasing to 200 times its normal value. The Zion-Mount Carmel Highway through the park was temporarily closed due to rocks and debris on the road, stranding some vehicles. The rain also caused an extended closure of the Angels Landing, Upper Emerald Pools, and Kayenta Trails. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bald Mountain Fire in northern Utah began on August 24 and burned for over a month before being contained. Note that this episode continued through September into  October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bald Mountain Fire was a lightning caused wildfire that began on August 24. Initially only about 20 acres, it experienced exceptional growth on September 12 due to high winds and low relative humidity, and eventually burned 18,620 acres. The Bald Mountain Fire, and nearby Pole Creek Fire that ignited in September, led to the evacuation of 6,000 people from 2,000 homes at the height of the incident, as well as caused numerous road closures. The combined costs to fight the Bald Mountain Fire and the Pole Creek Fire were $23.8 million. Note that this event continued through September into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Dollar Ridge Fire in northern Utah began on July 1 and burned for two months before being effectively contained. Note that this episode began in July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dollar Ridge Fire was a human caused wildfire that began during the afternoon of July 1. The fire burned 68,869 acres and destroyed 363 structures, including 74 homes. Costs to fight the fire were approximately $18 million, though rehabilitation efforts were projected to add significantly to the cost. At the height of the fire, approximately 1,100 people were evacuated from communities in Wasatch and Duchesne Counties. The fire was considered effectively contained on August 31, at 94% containment. Note that this event began in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Within a very broad upper trough a moist low pressure circulation moved across the region and produced significant snowfall in some areas of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches were measured across the zone.  Roads above the 5000 foot level became snowpacked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of 2018 saw the arrival of a strong cutoff low pressure system into the southern Great Basin. While the northern portion of the trough produced light snowfall over the northern mountains, the cutoff low produced moderate snowfall over the La Sal and Abajo Mountains as well as the lower elevations of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell in the La Sal and Abajo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low that slid through the Four Corners region produced snow showers for the higher elevations of southeastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.  Law enforcement reported Utah State Route 191 snowpacked and icy.  Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow which also resulted in heightened avalanche concerns.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 22 to 32 degrees F across the entire zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation and cooler than normal temperatures were experienced across much of eastern Utah in October. This allowed for some improvement in the state of the drought across portions of east-central and northeast Utah. However, there was no change in the drought status across far southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mid October saw an improvement in the state of the drought from extreme (D3) to severe (D2) across the southwest portion of the forecast zone. However, the extreme drought continued over the southeast portion of the forecast zone. On October 15th, the Governor of Utah declared a statewide drought emergency.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure remained quasi-stationary over the Great Basin and resulted in a moist south-southwesterly flow over eastern Utah. This led to some significant early season snowfall accumulations at the higher elevations in the La Sal and Abajo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 10 inches of snow was measured at the top of Geyser Pass with about 11 inches at the Camp Jackson SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation and cooler than normal temperatures were experienced across much of eastern Utah in October. Elsewhere, the extreme drought persisted through the end of the month. On October 15th, the Governor of Utah declared a statewide drought emergency.

However, the exceptional drought persisted across the far southeast perimeter through the end of the month. On October 15th, the Governor of Utah declared a statewide drought emergency.

The exceptional (D4) drought persisted over the southeast portion of the zone through the end of the month, though it was trimmed back slightly. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and quick-moving disturbance dropped into eastern Utah from the Pacific Northwest. This led to some moderate early season snowfall accumulations at the higher elevations of the Eastern Uinta Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent disturbance embedded in a southwest flow aloft produced strong thunderstorms which moved across east-central Utah. These storms produced heavy rain in a short amount of time which fell on saturated soils and led to some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in a rockslide with other debris that fell down onto Highway 128 and closed the road for about an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent disturbance embedded in a southwest flow aloft produced strong thunderstorms which moved across east-central Utah. These storms produced heavy rain in a short amount of time which fell on saturated soils and led to some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood due to heavy rainfall came down through Arches National Park and across the Delicate Arch Road.  The flowing water that was up to four feet deep closed the road for over an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bald Mountain Fire in northern Utah began on August 24 and burned for over a month before being contained. Note that this episode began in August and continued though September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bald Mountain Fire was a lightning caused wildfire that began on August 24. Initially only about 20 acres, it experienced exceptional growth on September 12 due to high winds and low relative humidity, and eventually burned 18,620 acres. The Bald Mountain Fire and nearby Pole Creek Fire led to the evacuation of 6,000 people from 2,000 homes at the height of the incident, as well as caused numerous road closures. The combined costs to fight the Bald Mountain Fire and the Pole Creek Fire were $23.8 million. The Bald Mountain Fire was contained on October 2. Note that this event began in August and continued through September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pole Creek Fire in northern Utah began on September 6 and burned for about a month before being contained. Note that this episode began in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pole Creek Fire was a lightning caused wildfire that began on September 6. On September 12, high winds and low relative humidity caused the fire to rapidly grow, and it eventually burned 102,190 acres. The Pole Creek Fire and nearby Bald Mountain Fire led to the evacuation of 6,000 people from 2,000 homes at the height of the incident, as well as caused numerous road closures The combined costs to fight the Pole Creek Fire and Bald Mountain Fire were $23.8 million. The Pole Creek Fire was contained on October 7. Note that this event began in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November 2018 was a great month for enhancing the snowpack as several disturbances moved across eastern Utah and produced significant early season snow accumulations. However, even with all the winter storms that moved into the region, November still ended up being drier than normal for the valleys. Nonetheless, several areas in northeast and southeast Utah saw a one category improvement in the drought classification.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional (D4) drought persisted across most of the zone through November, with some improvement to extreme (D3) in the western portion by the very end of the month.

However, extreme (D3) drought persisted across the majority of the zone through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through Utah bringing snow and travel incidents throughout Utah's valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts along the Southern Wasatch Front included 6.5 inches of snow in Lehi, 6 inches in American Fork, and 5 inches near Saratoga Springs. A daily snowfall record was set in Lehi. Snow covered roadways caused more than 70 crashes and slide-offs on I-15 in Juab County according to the Utah Highway Patrol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through Utah bringing snow and travel incidents throughout Utah's valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts in the Salt Lake Valley and Tooele Valley included 9 inches in Herriman, 5.9 inches at Salt Lake City International Airport, 6.5 inches in Sandy, and 9 inches in Tooele. Daily snowfall records were set at Tooele and Salt Lake City International Airport. According to the Utah Highway Patrol, 91 traffic accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved through Utah on New Year's Eve bringing snow on December 30 and early on the 31. After the snow, high winds associated with a downslope wind event occurred over the Northern Wasatch Front on the 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts observed include 71 mph at the US-89 at Park Lane sensor, 61 mph at Centerville, 60 mph at Farmington, and 60 mph at CENEWS. High winds caused trees to snap in Farmington, and more than 500 customers of Rocky Mountain Power lost power. High winds and blowing snow combined to create hazardous travel along I-15 between Bountiful and Weber Canyon, with reports of semitrailers blown over.
